title: sed
summary: sed (stream editor) processes text line-by-line, applying editing commands. It is most commonly used for search-and-replace operations in scripts, without opening a text editor. sed reads from files or stdin and writes to stdout by default.
keyPoints: s/pattern/replacement/: substitute first match on each line
s/pattern/replacement/g: substitute all matches on each line (global flag)
-i: edit file in place (modifies the actual file)
-n suppresses default output; use with p flag to print only matching lines
Patterns are Basic Regular Expressions (BRE) by default; use -E for ERE

exercises.prompt: Which sed command replaces the first occurrence of 'foo' with 'bar' on each line?
exercises.explanation: The s command syntax is s/pattern/replacement/flags. Without the g flag only the first match per line is replaced.

exercises.prompt: What flag makes sed replace all occurrences in a line, not just the first?
exercises.explanation: g stands for global. Without it, sed stops after the first match on each line.

exercises.prompt: Write a sed command that replaces every occurrence of 'localhost' with '127.0.0.1' in config.txt and saves the result back to the same file.
exercises.hint: Use the -i flag for in-place editing and g for all occurrences.
